statusLine: EU fuel security shows mixed signals with adequate average reserves but 11 countries below critical thresholds for petrol and all countries except three critically short on diesel.
fullAnalysis:
Despite an EU average of 104.4 days for petrol reserves—well above the 90-day minimum—fuel security remains precarious across member states. Eleven countries fall below the 90-day threshold for petrol, with Romania (47.8 days) and Luxembourg (48.5 days) in particularly dire situations. The diesel picture is more alarming: only Greece (64.9 days), Germany (63.9 days), and Czechia (73.5 days) maintain what could be considered adequate reserves, though all remain below the 90-day standard.

The complete absence of jet fuel reserves (0 days EU-wide) represents a critical vulnerability for aviation and military operations. This coincides with elevated Brent crude prices at $107.16 per barrel, though the recent $5.62 decline suggests some market stabilization after the 27% surge during the current Middle East conflict.

Fuel prices reflect the underlying supply tensions, with petrol averaging €1.682/L and diesel €1.824/L across the EU. The price disparity between countries is significant—Netherlands petrol costs 76% more than Bulgaria's—indicating uneven market access and varying national taxation policies during this supply-constrained period.
keyPoints: Romania and Luxembourg face severe fuel shortages with petrol reserves below 50 days, well under the 90-day EU minimum
Diesel reserves are critically low across all EU countries, with none meeting the 90-day standard and most below 75 days
Complete absence of jet fuel reserves poses immediate risks to aviation and defense capabilities
Brent crude remains elevated at $107.16/barrel despite recent 5% decline, reflecting ongoing Middle East supply disruptions
Major price variations across member states suggest uneven supply chain resilience, with Netherlands paying 76% more than Bulgaria for petrol
